text: The surgeon grasps the Bard 3-D Max mesh, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon enters the deep space above the transversalis fascia, maintaining a superficial plane to avoid the infraepigastric vessels.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers along the surface of the barred three-D max mesh, observing how the entire mesh structure moves as a single unit.

The surgeon carefully advances the scissors, maintaining awareness of the nearby vessels. The dissection creates a deep flap above the scissors, while the surgeon navigates immediately beneath the muscle layer.
The surgeon carefully cuts through the mesh, maintaining the incision directly on top of the mesh layer.

The surgeon moves through the surgical field, crossing over and sweeping tissue off the mesh while working medially. Gentle traction is applied to reveal the plane, avoiding excessive tearing of the tissue above the mesh.
The surgeon continues to work in the surgical field, crossing over tissue layers. Suture material becomes visible, followed by the appearance of metal tacks.
The surgeon works laterally, revealing nerve tissue and various debris, including mesh and suture material, in the surgical field.
The surgeon splits the mesh and begins dissection over the pubic tubercle, aiming to identify anatomical landmarks. Metal clips are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon sweeps debris off the mesh material, revealing metal tacks embedded within the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a marking pen to outline areas on the patient's abdomen where pain was indicated, while the assistant applies pressure on these marked spots. The mesh is visible on the left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates the right side of the surgical field, moving back and forth while carefully identifying anatomical landmarks amidst visible mesh and metal tacks.
The surgeon stays on the pubic bone, splitting the mesh to reveal metal tacks. The assistant applies pressure on a marked spot, located just superior to the metal tack.
The surgeon identifies and unscrews multiple metal tacks from the mesh material near the pubic bone, focusing on areas highlighted for pain relief.
The surgeon continues to remove tacks and metal clips from the mesh material located underneath the pubic bone, revealing multiple clips scattered throughout the area.

The surgeon carefully splits the mesh over the pubic bone, progressing slowly centimeter by centimeter to identify anatomical landmarks, while visible metal tacks and clips are present in the surgical field.
The surgeon removes multiple metal tacks and associated material from the surgical field, focusing on the area around the pubic bone.
The surgeon carefully peels the mesh off the raw surface near the iliac vessels, which appear distorted and pushed laterally due to previous mesh placement.
The surgeon encounters bleeding while navigating around clips on vessels, possibly related to previously repaired varicocele, with visible dilated veins extending intra-abdominally.
The surgeon removes a metal clip from the area beneath the pubic bone, which unexpectedly causes bleeding. The surgeon then sutures the site to control the bleeding.
The surgeon carefully sweeps and pushes the mesh down, revealing the underlying chord structures. Clips are visible laterally as the dissection progresses around the corner.
The surgeon observes suture material and clips within the surgical field, attempting to identify them amidst the absence of distinct anatomical structures, and begins working through the area.

The surgeon navigates through distorted anatomy, removing clips and encountering a vein. The iliac and spermatic cord are positioned away from the surgical field, allowing the surgeon to suture the vein closed.
The surgeon identifies a structure that appears to be a cord lipoma vein or a varicocele remnant and sutures it closed, successfully stopping the bleeding.

The surgeon continues working in the surgical field, focusing on the deep internal ring where two clips are visible on the ilioinguinal nerve. The nerve is seen coursing underneath the clips as the surgeon carefully removes them.

The surgeon carefully removes a clip from a nerve, previously mistaken for a blood vessel, tracking the nerve laterally. The surgeon continues to work from medial to lateral, releasing tissue as they progress.
The surgeon identifies small pieces of material resembling partially dissolved old Vicryl suture within the surgical field.
The surgeon is positioned directly above the spermatic cord, carefully peeling down the mesh while switching views to maintain visualization of the area.
The surgeon uses gentle cautery to sweep the fat down, revealing proximity to the iliac region.
The surgeon encounters mesh adhered to the iliac region and continues lateral dissection to deepen the pocket for mesh reapplication.

The surgeon identifies suture material deep within the tissue plane and sweeps surrounding tissue away from the iliac vein, visible on the right side of the surgical field. Confident in the vein's position, the surgeon proceeds with tissue manipulation.
The surgeon sweeps tissue medially, clearing the surgical field around the iliac vein and spermatic cord, while removing remnants of old suture material.
The surgeon prepares to place an extra-large three max mesh, extending the surgical pocket laterally to accommodate the mesh size.
The surgeon creates a lateral pocket to ensure the mesh lays flat without scrunching, carefully peeling off the mesh that is stuck.
The surgeon removes the mesh from the marked area beneath the clips, which was identified as a high-intensity pain region. The lateral mesh fixation is carefully extracted from the tissue.
The surgeon cleans the muscle, removing the remaining mesh. The surgical area appears well-prepared, with the mesh fitting appropriately.
